@graph.name: Phreaking Collective
@graph.description: Phreaking Collective is a London-based arts organisation presenting talks, workshops, performances, and screenings exploring digital art, computational art, AI art, live coding, new media, and internet culture.

@graph.name: Past Events — Phreaking Collective
@graph.description: Archive of past talks, workshops, performances, and screenings from Phreaking Collective exhibitions including BitRot and Does Cloud Compute (ever) Precipitate?.
@graph.keywords: digital art, computational art, AI art, live coding, algorithmic art, experimental media, creative coding, London art events, new media art, media archaeology, critical AI

@graph.name: Past Events
@graph.description: Archive of past talks, workshops, performances, and screenings from Phreaking Collective.

@graph.name: BitRot
@graph.description: Our inaugural show in Copeland Gallery on decay, entropy, and the poetry of corrupted data. A multi-room show featuring installations, type experiments, and audiovisual pieces from the collective.

@graph.name: Does Cloud Compute (ever) Precipitate?
@graph.description: A question about hidden infrastructures, energy, and the weather systems of data. Installations, sound, and computational works translate the cloud into something tangible.

@graph.name: Lucia Rebolino
@graph.description: Artist working with web performance, climate modelling aesthetics, digital architecture, and computational realism.

@graph.name: Terence Broad
@graph.description: Artist and researcher exploring AI as artistic material, generative systems, and critical approaches to machine learning in creative practice.

@graph.name: Robin Leverton
@graph.description: Artist exploring internet infrastructure, digital geographies, and communications networks.

@graph.name: Erin Robinson
@graph.description: Artist whose practice spans digital embodiment, artificial intimacy, and critical theory, co-creator of the award-winning work XXX Machina.

@graph.name: Anthony Frisby
@graph.description: Artist exploring eroticism, selfhood, and computational desire, co-creator of the award-winning work XXX Machina.

@graph.name: Dian Joy
@graph.description: Artist and writer working with media theory, digital folklore, platform culture, and algorithmic desire.

@graph.name: Kat Macdonald
@graph.description: Artist exploring collective sound making, experimental performance, and the history of London's experimental collective performance scene.

@graph.name: Cranberry Lemonade
@graph.description: Artist and educator running beginner-friendly web-making workshops covering HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for personal and poetic webpages.

@graph.name: Matt Hill
@graph.description: Artist exploring file formats, permacomputing, bytebeats, generative image-making, and Unicode-art.

@graph.name: Margot McEwen
@graph.description: Artist facilitating workshops in creative coding, WebGL, live coding tools, and interactive visual systems for artists.

@graph.name: Melissa Li Yunzhi
@graph.description: Artist working with object-oriented storytelling, bridging physical material and virtual worlds, creator of Home is a Dwelling Path.

@graph.name: Lagtime Seedling
@graph.description: Performer who creates audio-visual compositions made entirely within web browser windows using live YouTube video tabs and a virtual piano, without traditional music software.

@graph.name: Deposit Silk
@graph.description: Performer who appeared at Phreaking Collective's Does Cloud Compute (ever) Precipitate? exhibition, London, 2026.

@graph.name: Yiding Zhang
@graph.description: Performer and artist whose live practice uses a custom-built modular synthesizer.

@graph.name: Orangespy
@graph.description: Performer integrating real-time audio-visual systems, algorithmic composition, AI-driven generative visuals, and sensor-based interactivity.

@graph.name: That Sounds Exciting!
@graph.description: Live coding performance group who performed at Phreaking Collective's Does Cloud Compute (ever) Precipitate? exhibition, London, 2026.

@graph.name: Claire McMichael
@graph.description: Performer who appeared at Phreaking Collective's Does Cloud Compute (ever) Precipitate? exhibition, London, 2026.

@graph.name: Algorave London
@graph.description: London live coding collective performing algorithmic and generative music and visuals, part of the global Algorave movement.

@graph.name: Counter-Modelling
@graph.label: Talk
@graph.description: A web-based performance exploring climate modelling through digital aesthetics and architectural thinking. Moving between early numerical forecasting and contemporary synthetic intelligence, Counter-Modelling challenged the aesthetics of computational realism, presenting climate prediction as a site of tension between technological development and climate futures.
@graph.keywords: climate modelling, digital aesthetics, computational realism, web performance, AI, architecture

@graph.name: AI as Material
@graph.label: Lecture

images.alt: Terence Broad presenting AI as Material

@graph.description: A talk showing how practices that treat AI as a material rather than a tool or creative agent allow artists to make critical works presenting new ways of understanding unfathomably complex computational systems.
@graph.keywords: AI as material, machine learning, critical AI, generative art, computational art

@graph.name: Cloud-Watching Through My Window
@graph.label: Talk

images.alt: Cloud-Watching Through My Window talk poster

@graph.description: A flâneur's guide to mapping the new geographies of connection, exploring internet and communications infrastructures, physical and digital topographies, and contemporary data emissions.
@graph.keywords: internet infrastructure, digital geography, network culture, data, cloud computing

@graph.name: Artificial Intimacies & Erotics of the Synthetic Self: Making XXX Machina
@graph.description: A lecture exploring the making of XXX Machina, tracing its development across philosophy, technical process, and lived experience, discussing artificial intimacies, eroticism, and digital embodiment.
@graph.keywords: artificial intimacy, digital embodiment, post-structuralism, postmodernism, computational desire, XXX Machina

@graph.name: Long Live The New Flesh (or Whatever)
@graph.description: A lecture exploring how desire functions as a mechanism of discipline and ideological capture within the contemporary digital landscape, drawing from media theory, philosophy, and digital folklore, with a close reading of Videodrome.
@graph.keywords: media theory, digital folklore, desire, platform culture, algorithmic culture, Videodrome, meme culture

@graph.name: Noisy Spaces
@graph.label: Workshop
@graph.description: A workshop exploring technology as it relates to collective sound making, tracing London's experimental collective performance scene of the 1970s and how those methods apply to current art culture.
@graph.keywords: sound art, collective performance, experimental music, workshop, London, 1970s

@graph.name: I Want To Be A Website
@graph.label: Workshop

I Want To Be A Website workshop detail

@graph.description: A hands-on, beginner-friendly personal web-making workshop exploring HTML, CSS and JavaScript to create personalised and poetic webpages and tell digital stories.

@graph.name: Phreaking Film Night
@graph.label: Screening
@graph.description: An evening of computational, experimental, and artist-led short films bending narrative, fracturing reality, and playing at the edges of code, image, and memory. Featuring films by Lucy Ellis, Autojektor, Atay Ilgun, Maggie Maggot, Erinne Huston, Dana Fox, Shannon Lu, Verona Gryshchuk, Max Colson, Natalie Maximova, Isaac Ward, Tilly Hawkins (Nukleopatra), Ned Caderni, Iman Javaid and Julian Law, Ngo Chun Phoenix Tse, Mati Granica, and Yidi Chen.
@graph.keywords: experimental film, artist film, short film, computational art, screening, London

@graph.label: Event

@graph.keywords: open projector, artist presentations, project sharing, community, Does Cloud Compute

@graph.name: Closing Event

@graph.description: The closing event of Does Cloud Compute (ever) Precipitate? featured a panel discussion with participating artists and curators, followed by a line-up of live-coding performances by London live coding groups including That Sounds Exciting!, Algorave London, Lagtime Seedling, Deposit Silk, and Claire McMichael.
@graph.keywords: closing event, panel discussion, live coding, performance, Does Cloud Compute

@graph.name: Slow Impulse
@graph.label: Experimental Performance Night
@graph.description: Slow Impulse will be an evening of experimental sound night that journeys through traditional, analogue, and technological ways of sound making. Join us in a large school hall for this very exciting, beautiful, and immersive sound night!

@graph.name: Phreak Mag Launch Party #2
@graph.label: Event
@graph.description: Join us to celebrate the launch of issue #2 of Phreak Mag. Hear from the magazine editor, and a panel talk with selected writers and artists from the magazine

@graph.name: Mixed Signals
@graph.label: Workshop
@graph.description: A monthly art jam where participants are given a random computational material, process, and subject and have only a set time to create artwork. The sessions encourage collaboration and experimental approaches to digital tools.

@graph.name: Atay Ilgun - spiral
@graph.label: Talk
@graph.description: Taking his artistic practice spanning early AI, crypto and protocol art, this talk thinks through cultural time and the hype cycle, and takes the spiral as a counterform that reads emergence and obsolescence together. Network and protocol art are proposed as forms whose internal time is structurally different: work that operates from inside its infrastructure and holds its prior states forward as material. Rather than a break from market-dependent digital culture, this is a reality hack within it, working on the rules that decide what is real. It moves through Otherness and the ./spiral exhibition, co-curated with jonooo on SuperRare, alongside soft world, a curatorial studio for protocol art: instances of a shift toward a quieter, more durable version of what digital culture could still become.

@graph.name: Nina Davies
@graph.label: Talk
@graph.description: Speed Running the World of Things. As part of this programme Nina Davies opens up her research in progress, presenting it as a fragmented talk. Opening with teenagers speedrunning a Scientology building, she traces a series of tangents through Boston Dynamics' dancing robots, NASA's 'riding the skin' VR experiments, and the world models that teach physical AI to rehearse being in our world before it arrives. Drawing on several years of research into what happens to human movement when it leaves the body, the talk arrives at a single question: as physical AI inherits human movement to navigate a human-built world, what new forms of embodied literacy must we develop to navigate it in return?

@graph.name: Worldbuilding as Resistance: Creating 3D Sanctuaries
@graph.label: Workshop
@graph.eventCompletedUrl: Worldbuilding as Resistance: Creating 3D Sanctuaries with LiBi Engine
@graph.description: In a digital world dominated by corporate platforms and surveillance, worldbuilding is an act of resistance. Join this hands-on workshop and reclaim the web by creating your own sovereign 3D sanctuaries — virtual art galleries, personal rooms, tree-lined worlds, or tranquil escapes that are yours to own, shape, and share with just a link. Using the browser-based Libi Engine, you will worldbuild at a personal scale: bring your own assets or start with ready-made models, then decorate and personalise your space with lighting, atmosphere, audio, and meaningful details. No prior experience is required — just bring curiosity and a laptop (a mouse is recommended). By the end of this two-hour workshop you will have a fully accessible, standalone 3D world that can be shared through the web. Hosted by Ameer Ali and Ericka Simon.

@graph.name: Olivia Ema
@graph.label: Talk
@graph.description:
Olivia Ema is a lecturer at University of the Arts London, a digital artist, and founder of a creative technology studio. She shares her observations on how interactive digital art and technology shape the way we perceive space, ourselves, and each other.

Speaking as a Black woman working in creative technology, the talk reflects on how identity and intuition shape the systems and worlds we build. It explores how digital systems, built using interactive software like Touchdesigner, can hold presence, respond to a room, and shift something in people almost without them noticing.

Drawing on Olivia's projects, including an interactive floor installation reflecting on grief and large-scale installations across London from Outernet to Piccadilly, the session moves through both the technical process and what's actually driving it underneath.

@graph.name: Metel Upa + Jin / Opening Drinks & Social
@graph.label: Performance
@graph.description: Opening drinks and social with performances by Metel Upa and Jin.

@graph.name: Encrypted Embroidery
@graph.label: Workshop
@graph.description:
Encrypted Embroidery is a design system supported by the Data Reclamation Handbook both created by Sophie Nadel. 

In an increasingly all encompassing technology environment, the practice of encrypting information in embroidery encourages users to store their information offline. Not only does embroidery allow for slow, meditative creative practice, it also recentres a craft historically dismissed as ‘women’s work’. 

Do you have an address important to you? A password or secret phrase? Translate it into a hidden code and embroider it onto your belongings to keep it with you but away from prying eyes that aim to hold all your data.

@graph.name: Ringtone Workshop
@graph.label: Workshop
@graph.description: When did you last hear your ringtone? Artist Kat Macdonald leads participants through the history of ringtone customisation and the politics of sound in the commons. Using the Ring Tone Text Transfer Language (RTTTL), developed by Nokia in 1996 as a method of making and easily sharing ringtones between early mobile phones, participants make their own shareable ringtones. Bring your laptop!

@graph.name: No Notes: Making Music with SyntÉ
@graph.label: Workshop
@graph.description: What is SyntÉ and how do you make music without notes?! We take over our machines by delving into their software guts, then install and play with SyntÉ, a live coding language for sound. Please bring a laptop if you can; headphones are helpful but not necessary. Led by Daniel.

@graph.name: Cranberry Lemonade — Hyperlink Workshop
@graph.label: Workshop
@graph.description: A hyperlink workshop with Cranberry Lemonade.

@graph.name: Melissa Yunzhi Li
@graph.label: Talk
@graph.description: From Concrete to Cyberspace. In this 30-minute performance lecture, Melissa explores how urban architecture from the 1990s and digital platforms from the 2000s meet identity formation and social needs in Georgia, while navigating through a 3D built world.

@graph.name: Marcus Round
@graph.label: Talk
@graph.description: A performance lecture on his tarot cards, AI, and life and death.

@graph.name: Tiziana Alocci
@graph.label: Talk
@graph.description: Designing Futures Through Absence of Data. As our digital systems increasingly optimise for speed, extraction, prediction, and surveillance, what happens to everything they cannot measure? In this talk, data artist and lecturer Tiziana Alocci argues why the absence of data — or what is not counted — matters, sometimes even more than what is recorded. Drawing on projects that transform invisible data such as soundscapes, personal testimonies, found online data, and electromagnetic fields into data-driven installations, she explores how alternative forms of data collection can challenge dominant technological narratives and reveal what contemporary systems routinely overlook.
